segments.s004-g25: نه begins a negative clause. گشت (here from گشتن) is used as a noun/verb phrase 'the turning / passing' of زمانه ('the time, the age'); word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect. بفرسایدش is a present/ordinary-form verb with the prefix ب- and the enclitic -ش 'it' (object), meaning 'wear away / consume' in context; together the clause means 'the passage of time does not wear it away.'
segments.s004-g26: نه again negates the parallel clause. آن رنج و تیمار is a coordinated noun phrase 'that pain and care'; بگزایدش is a third-person verb with prefix ب- plus enclitic -ش 'it'. In classical/poetic Persian گزاید in this context functions as a verb meaning to bring about/destroy or 'cause to affect', so the clause reads 'nor did that pain and care bring it to ruin.'
segments.s004-g27: نه negates the clause. از جنبش is a prepositional phrase 'from motion'; آرام is the state 'calm/peace'; گیرد (present tense) here means 'takes/receives' and together with همی (a classical habitual/continuous marker) gives an ongoing sense: 'it does not obtain calm from motion.' Note the verb-final tendency and the poetic placement of همی.
segments.s004-g28: The second negative clause parallels the first. چون ما = 'as/like us'; تباهی is the object 'destruction/ruin'; پذیرد is a present/habitual verb 'accepts/takes on' and again is followed by همی marking continuous/habitual aspect. The clause means 'nor does it, like us, take on ruin.'
segments.s004-g29: دان is an imperative 'know' (addressing the listener). از او introduces the source 'from him'; فزونی is the nominal thing to be known 'increase/abundance'. The second clause fragment از او هم شمار is elliptical: 'from him, too, (is) the count/tally' — هم 'also' links the second noun شمار to the same source. This is typical concise/elliptical classical Persian phrasing.
segments.s004-g30: بد و نیک is a coordinated noun phrase 'bad and good'; نزدیک او is a prepositional/relational phrase 'near him'; آشکار is a predicative adjective 'clear/manifest'. Word order places the predicate after the locative phrase for emphasis: 'bad and good are near him and manifest.'
segments.s004-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. زین ('this saddle') is the object of بگذری; بگذری is 2nd-person singular present (or present-subjunctive) of گذشتن 'to pass' and sets the temporal clause. مردم (subject) + آمد (past tense 'came') + پدید (an adverb/secondary predicate 'forth/appeared') is a compact poetic clause meaning 'people came forth'. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: temporal clause → subject → verb → adverbial.
segments.s004-g2: شد is past/correlative 'became' (copular/verb of change). این بندها را places 'these stanzas' with the object/topic marker را in front for emphasis (poetic inversion). سراسر کلید is a noun phrase 'a key throughout' (سراسر modifying کلید) — together: 'these stanzas became the key for the whole.' The clause lacks an explicit subject because of the topicalized object and poetic economy.
segments.s004-g3: سرش = سر + possessive ش 'his head'. راست بر شد is a light-verb construction: راست (straight/upright) + بر شد (بر + شد 'rose up/became upon') yielding 'rose/stood upright'. چو introduces a simile 'like', and سرو بلند is the simile's noun + adjective order 'a tall cypress'. The verb شد is past and completes the predication.
segments.s004-g4: به is a preposition 'in/with/by'. گفتار خوب is a noun + adjective 'good speech'. و links the next phrase. خرد کار بند is compact/poetic: خرد کار = 'wise deed/action' (noun + noun in close apposition), and بند (here) is used metaphorically as 'bound/restrained' (a noun 'fetter' used adjectivally) to mean 'restrained/governed by'. There is no explicit copula 'است' — the predicate quality is asserted directly in poetic style.
segments.s004-g5: پذیرنده is a participial/adjectival form 'receptive' or 'one who receives'. It governs a series of abstract nouns joined by و: هوش (intellect/awareness), رای (counsel/plan) and خرد (wisdom). The phrase lists the mental qualities that the person accepts; word order is noun cluster after the participle as typical in Persian.
segments.s004-g6: This short clause is compact and somewhat ambiguous in isolation. مر here is the archaic form meaning 'to me'/'me'; او را is a third-person object 'him' (marked by را); دد و دام is a compound noun phrase 'enemy and snare/trap'; فرمان برد is a verbal phrase from فرمان بردن (poetic past), meaning roughly 'took/obeyed (the) command' or 'carried out the command'. The line strings indirect objects and nouns in poetic order; because of elliptical/archaic syntax the precise argument structure (who commands whom) is compressed and context-dependent.
segments.s004-g7: ز is a poetic preposition 'from'. راه خرد is a genitive/compound 'the way of wisdom' (راه + خرد). بنگری is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of نگریستن used as an exhortation 'you should look/consider'; اندکی is an adverb 'a little/briefly'. The sequence is an instruction: 'from the way of wisdom, look a little.'
segments.s004-g8: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/so that' or an indirect question. مردم is the subject 'people'. به معنی is a prepositional phrase 'in the sense/meaning'. چه is an interrogative 'what', and باشد is a 3rd-person present-subjunctive/optative of بودن ('may be/is') used here in indirect question style. یکی is the word being discussed 'one' (the numeral/pronoun). The clause asks you to consider 'what people mean by
segments.s004-g9: مگر opens a rhetorical question here (“could it be that…?”). مردمی is the object 'the people' (in the indefinite/collective sense). خیره is an adjectival/adverbial descriptor 'staring' modifying the action. خوانی is 2nd‑person singular present 'you read/interpret', and همی is the archaic present/progressive particle (emphatic — ‘always/still’), so the clause literally reads “Are you then always reading the people while staring?”
segments.s004-g10: جز این را is ‘nothing/none but this’ with را marking the definite object. نشانی is a noun 'sign/indication'. ندانی is the 2nd‑person present negative of دانستن ('you do not know'); here it asserts ignorance. همی again adds the habitual/present shade. The whole clause: 'You know no sign except this.'
segments.s004-g11: تو را is the object 'you' with the object marker را. از دو گیتی literally 'out of/from the two worlds' (two realms). برآورده‌اند is a compound past tense (آورده‌اند with the verbal prefix بر‑) meaning 'they have brought/lifted up' — third plural perfect. The clause says they have 'brought you forth/up from the two worlds.'
segments.s004-g12: به چندین میانچی uses به + agent/instrument to indicate 'by/through many go‑betweens' (چندین = several). بپرورده‌اند is perfective 'they have reared/fostered' (ب‑ is the intensive/iterative prefix). Together: 'and they have reared you by many intermediaries.'
segments.s004-g13: نخستین فطرت is 'the first/original nature' (نخستین = first; فطرت = innate nature). پسین شمار is an imperative use of شمار (here 'count/consider later things second' — i.e. put later matters after the original nature). In short: 'Put your primary, innate nature first; count/regard the later second.'
segments.s004-g14: تویی is the emphatic copula 'thou art' (addressing the listener). خویشتن را is the reflexive object 'yourself' (را marks it). به بازی is an adverbial phrase 'as a play / for sport'. مدار here is an archaic negative admonition 'do not make/treat' (used as a prohibitive); the printed lemma senses are nouns, so the exact verbal sense is archaic/poetic. The clause means 'You are — do not treat yourself as a toy/plaything.'
segments.s004-g15: شنیدم is first‑person past 'I heard'. ز دانا is the prepositional phrase 'from the wise one'. دگرگونه زین means 'different from this' (دگرگونه = different; here زین functions as 'from this' in a classical fused form). The clause: 'I heard from a wise man something different from this.'
segments.s004-g16: چه دانیم is a rhetorical/questioning construction 'how shall we know' (چه here ≈ 'how' or 'in what way'). راز is 'secret/mystery' and جهان‌آفرین 'the world‑creator' (title for God). The clause asks rhetorically, 'how can we know the secret of the Creator of the world?'
segments.s004-g17: نگه کن ... ببین is a pair of imperatives: نگه کن (literally “do/look”) is an exhortation “take heed/look,” followed by the verb ببین “see” which again issues the instruction to observe. سرانجام خود را is the definite object (سرانجام = “end/outcome,” خود = reflexive “yourself/your own”) marked by را before the final verb; this word order (object before verb) is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s004-g18: چو introduces a conditional ('if'); بیابی is a subjunctive/potential form meaning 'you may find' (here signaling the conditional result). کاری here means ‘a thing/any alternative’ (sense list did not include that exact meaning). از این literally 'from/than this' (marking the thing compared to 'this'), and به گزین is an exhortation 'choose it,' with گزین functioning as an imperative.
segments.s004-g19: به رنج اندر آری is a verbal phrase meaning 'bring/put (yourself) into suffering/toil' (به ... اندر + verb = 'into/within'); تنت را is the object 'your body' marked by را. The sentence ends with رواست (روا + است) 'it is fitting/allowed' — a predicate evaluating the foregoing action. Note: آری here is an older/poetic verbal form (you put/bring) rather than the interjection 'yes.'
segments.s004-g20: که introduces the explanatory/casual clause 'for/because.' خود رنج بردن is a nominalized verb phrase ('the bearing/enduring of suffering itself') where بردن appears as the infinitive/masdar-like form 'to endure.' به دانش marks purpose/aim 'for knowledge.' سزاست (سزا + است) is a fixed predicate 'it is deserved/appropriate.'
segments.s004-g21: چو خواهی که یابی is a conditional clause: چو = 'if/when,' خواهی = 'you wish/want,' and یابی is a subjunctive/potential 'you may find' (subordinate after خواهی/که). ز هر بد رها is a prepositional phrase 'free from every evil' (ز = 'from,' هر بد = 'every ill,' رها = 'released/free').
segments.s004-g22: سر اندر نیاری is an admonition meaning roughly 'do not put your head (yourself) into...' — سر = 'head/self,' and نیاری here is an archaic/poetic negative jussive/imperative form meaning 'you should not put/bring' (the exact lemma given in the list does not match this verbal use). به دام بلا = 'into the snare/trap of calamity' (به marks direction into the trap; دام = 'trap'; بلا = 'calamity').
segments.s004-g23: نگه کن is the imperative 'look/pay attention.' بدین is the poetic contraction of به + این ('to/at this'); here it directs the addressee to the following noun. گنبد تیزگرد = 'the swift-rotating dome' (گنبد 'dome/sky'; تیزگرد an adjective 'swift-rolling/quickly turning'), with adjective after the noun as common in Persian poetry.
segments.s004-g24: که introduces explanation: درمان ازوی است = 'the cure/remedy is from him' (درمان = 'remedy,' از وی = 'from him,' است = copula). و زویست درد inverts normal word order for poetic effect: ز وی است درد = 'and the pain is also from him' (زویست = 'is from him,' درد = 'pain'). Both ازوی and زویست are prepositional + pronoun forms meaning 'from him.'

sentences.s004-s13: Neither did the turning of time wear it away, nor did that pain and care cause its undoing.
sentences.s004-s14: It gains no calm from motion, nor, like us, does it accept destruction.
sentences.s004-s15: Know that increase is from him; from him also comes the tally — bad and good are near him and manifest.
sentences.s004-s1: When you pass this saddle, people suddenly appeared; these stanzas became the key throughout.
sentences.s004-s2: His head rose straight up like a tall cypress; in good speech and wise deed he was restrained.
sentences.s004-s3: Receptive of intellect, counsel, and wisdom, — to/for me he, the enemy and the snare, obeyed the command.
sentences.s004-s4: Consider briefly, from the path of wisdom, how the people understand what 'one' means.
sentences.s004-s5: Are you perhaps always staring at the people and reading them? You know no sign other than this.
sentences.s004-s6: They have brought you up out of the two worlds and reared you by many intermediaries.
sentences.s004-s7: First consider your original nature; do not treat yourself as a play.
sentences.s004-s8: I heard from the wise something different from this; how can we know the secret of the World‑Creator?
sentences.s004-s9: Take heed: look at your own end; if you find something better than this, choose it.
sentences.s004-s10: It is fitting that you subject your body to toil, for enduring hardship oneself is deserved for the sake of knowledge.
sentences.s004-s11: If you wish to be freed from every evil, do not put your head into the snare of calamity.
sentences.s004-s12: Look at this swift-rolling dome: its remedy comes from him, and the pain also is from him.
